text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Environmental and Resource Impacts lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI can carry energy, water, hardware, e-waste, procurement, and environmental justice implications that should be considered before scale.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI can carry energy, water, hardware, e-waste, procurement, and environmental justice implications that should be considered before scale.
textPreview: AI can carry energy, water, hardware, e-waste, procurement, and environmental justice implications that should be considered before scale.

text: AI systems can have energy, water, hardware, cost, and environmental justice implications. Departments should consider whether the public health value of a high-compute use justifies its resource burden and whether lower-impact alternatives.

text: High-compute AI uses should be justified by public health value and reviewed for resource burden and environmental justice implications.

text:

Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 8 and Tools 5, 30, 33.
Emphasize the core guardrail: High-compute AI uses should be justified by public health value and reviewed for resource burden and environmental justice implications.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Environmental and Resource Impacts in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Environmental and Resource Impacts training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 8 and Tools 5, 30, 33.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI systems can require substantial compute, energy, water, hardware, and vendor infrastructure. Public health departments do not need to perform complex environmental modeling for every use, but they should consider whether a proposed AI use is proportionate to the public health value, whether lower-impact alternatives exist, and whether infrastructure burdens raise environmental justice concerns.

text: Ask vendors for sustainability, compute, hosting, and data center information when relevant. Compare lower-impact model sizes, retrieval approaches, or existing enterprise tools. Include resource impacts in funding and sustainment planning.

text: Higher resource burden should be justified by clear public health value, governance approval, and feasible sustainment. Environmental review should be documented for high-compute or large-scale deployments.

Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 7, 8 and Tools 5, 30, 33.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Is AI necessary for this task, or would a simpler approach work? What sustainability and infrastructure information should vendors disclose? Could resource burdens affect communities already facing environmental inequities?

text: Review one proposed AI project and compare a high-compute option, a smaller model or RAG option, and a non-AI option. Document benefits, limitations, costs, and resource implications.
